text: The surgeon repositions the fourth robotic arm to enhance exposure of the vesicles, adjusting the fibers for optimal visualization.
The surgeon holds the deferent and performs a medial dissection with a vascular plan to expose the vesicles, which are notably large in this patient.
The surgeon avoids using cautery while navigating the complex contact between the vesicle and gastric structures, focusing on minimizing thermal damage during dissection.

The surgeon completes dissection of the left vesicle and holds the right deferent to develop the medial plane, focusing on the seminal vesicle plane. The seminal vesicle is identified before connecting the deferent, noting the large size of the vesicle.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to manipulate and expose the large viscera during dissection, avoiding the use of cautery. The surgeon repositions to access the axis of the viscera for clipping, emphasizing the importance of traction and exposure for successful removal.
The surgeon clips the final vesicle pedicle, with visible large vesicles requiring careful handling. A pedicle artery is identified and clipped, despite a stain on the camera lens.
The surgeon begins the preservation of neurovascular tissue, searching for an avascular interfacial plane, which is typically visible in most patients.

The surgeon carefully dissects below the dorsal venous plexus to preserve it and avoid bleeding, maneuvering under the fibers of the dorsal plexus veins. The retrograde preservation technique involves connecting the lateral dissection plane with the posterior plane, while cautiously navigating around veins to prevent significant bleeding.

The surgeon carefully connects the dissection planes, aiming for an avascular dissection with minimal bleeding. The tissue on one side appears slightly bent, and the boundary of the dissection area is clearly visible.

The surgeon attempts to enter under the dorsal venous plexus during dissection, aiming to avoid significant bleeding. The surgical field is tight, particularly on the right side, where the pedicle is visible.
The surgeon identifies the PI-REDS-IV lesion at the right base of the prostate and leaves a margin of fat tissue to avoid a positive surgical margin, due to the high disease presence in this area.

The surgeon leaves a small margin of fat at the base to avoid a positive margin, then attempts to connect the dissection planes at the apex, which are closely adhered. The surgeon carefully maneuvers to reach the apex, indicating progress in connecting the planes.

The surgeon successfully connects the lateral dissection plane with the posterior plane, completing the dissection around the prostate. The prostate pedicle remains to be addressed, with a larger margin left on the side with the greatest amount of disease.

The surgeon preserves the closed area at the right base due to the presence of the vascular pedicle, indicating completion of dissection on this side. The left side is anticipated to be easier to dissect.
The surgeon binds the median pedicle using a surgical brush, facilitating the rolling of the tissue for easier manipulation.
The surgeon attempts to use tweezers to go under the cleft, aiming to visualize both sides and assess significant bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to navigate under the 'D' structure to preserve it, using a duplex approach in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully identifies a low plane near the prostate, ensuring proper preservation of the surrounding veins during this challenging part of the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon enters under the plexus vein, gently pushing the veins aside to expose the surgical field.

The surgeon gradually releases the anus plexus while preserving its upward orientation. Attempts are made to rotate the clamp, which is visibly challenging, affecting the manipulation of the left side.

The surgeon adjusts pins to improve access and begins releasing the dorsal plexus, searching for a vascular plane between the plexus fibers. The vascular plane becomes visible as the surgeon manipulates the tissue closer to the plexus.

The surgeon performs a retrograde dissection on the left side, which appears more loose due to the absence of significant disease. The area is visibly communicated, possibly indicating previous biopsy sites.

The surgeon carefully dissects the neurovascular tissue while preserving the adjuvant plexus, entering a distinct plane between the plexus and prostate that minimizes bleeding. The retina is visibly exposed during this process.
The surgeon delineates the urethra while releasing the flexion and prostatic axis on the right side, showing a relaxed surgical field without connection to the adjuvant plexus.
The surgeon identifies a vesicle polyp adjacent to bladder fibers and clips the artery supplying the polyp to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon controls the prostate, releasing it from surrounding tissues, while the liver plan is completely exposed. The pedicle is identified for clipping, with care taken to preserve the muscular liver after completing the lateral dissection.
The surgeon clips the base of the prostate, which is adhered, and carefully manipulates the tissue to facilitate its passage towards the end of the dissection.

The surgeon places a suture on the vesicle collar to close it slightly. The right side appears more adhered, with some tissue left on the right base due to a higher volume of disease. The surgeon uses a preferred suture thread to ensure proper closure.

The surgeon begins the posterior reconstruction during the prostatectomy, using a thread with two needles to reduce tension at the anastomosis site. The cutting edge of the anastomosis is visible, and perineal traction is applied without an aspirator.

The surgeon places a posterior suture point behind the urethra, visible at the back of the skin. This point is intended to relieve pressure and can be removed later. The edge of the dendroplasia is visible, and an aspirator is used during the reconstruction process.

The surgeon places a posterior suture on the back of the skin and along the edge of the dendroplasia, bringing the tissues closer together. The surgical field is observed without any aspiration devices in use.
The surgeon uses a second needle to perform the colovesical suture, carefully suturing the posterior urethra to the colovesical junction.
The surgeon performs a double anastomosis, bringing the bladder closer to the rectum, ensuring a secure connection between these structures.
The surgeon performs a posterior anastomosis, using wires with scales that visibly draw closer together. The use of monofilament wire is noted to be more challenging.
The surgeon completes the posterior reconstruction, ensuring the tension is manageable in preparation for the subsequent anastomosis.

The surgeon ties two wires together, one from the bottom and one from the posterior reconstruction, to approximate the tissues. The process requires careful attention as the piece does not easily align, ensuring ease in subsequent steps.
